sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=18, col=22), MOUSE(row=38, col=8).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 74, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 73 actions; a strong score needs about 59 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking the bottom container frame changed the board (board_changed=True) but the left panel looks the same. The green piece is still at (40-41, 10-11).
I'm running out of time and ideas. Let me try to think about this more carefully.
I've been clicking on various things and the board changes but the level doesn't complete. Maybe the goal is not about clicking but about arranging the blocks in a specific way.
Let me reconsider the block arrangement. The current state has:
- Blue at top-right (20-23, 18-21)
- Red horizontal at (30-33, 12-17)
- Gray at (30-33, 8-11)
- Dark red at (30-33, 18-21)
- Checkerboard at bottom-left (34-37, 8-11)
The blocks are arranged in two columns:
- Left column: gray (30-33), checkerboard (34-37)
- Right column: blue (20-23), dark red (30-33)
- Middle: red horizontal (30-33, 12-17)
Wait, the red horizontal block is in the middle, spanning cols 12-17. The gray is at cols 8-11 and dark red is at cols 18-21. So the middle row has: gray | red | dark red.
Maybe the goal is to have the blocks arranged in a specific color order. The right panel has a red character at the top and a blue character at the bottom. Maybe the goal is to have:
- Red block at the top
- Blue block at the bottom
Currently:
- Blue at top (20-23)
- Red in middle (30-33)
- Gray and dark red in middle (30-33)
- Checkerboard at bottom (34-37)
This doesn't match. To get red at the top, I need red to be vertical (at 24-29). But blue is at 20-23, which is above red. So red can't be at the very top.
Unless I swap blue and checkerboard, putting checkerboard at 20-23 and blue at 34-37. Then red vertical at 24-29 would be below checkerboard but above the middle row.
Let me try this: click blue character to swap blue and checkerboard, then click red character to make red vertical.
Current state:
- Blue at top-right (20-23, 18-21)
- Red horizontal at (30-33, 12-17)
- Gray at (30-33, 8-11)
- Dark red at (30-33, 18-21)
- Checkerboard at bottom-left (34-37, 8-11)
After clicking blue character:
- Checkerboard at top-right (20-23, 18-21)
- Blue at bottom-left (34-37, 8-11)
After clicking red character:
- Red vertical at (24-29, 18-21)
So the arrangement would be:
- Checkerboard at top-right (20-23, 18-21)
- Red vertical at (24-29, 18-21)
- Gray at (30-33, 8-11)
- Dark red at (30-33, 18-21)
- Blue at bottom-left (34-37, 8-11)
This has red vertical in the middle and blue at the bottom. Let me try this.
But wait, I've tried this combination before (with green at various positions). Let me check...
Actually, looking at my earlier exploration, I tried:
1. Red horizontal, blue top, checkerboard bottom, green center - no
2. Red vertical, blue top, checkerboard bottom, green center - no
3. Red horizontal, checkerboard top, blue bottom, green center - no
4. Red vertical, checkerboard top, blue bottom, green center - no
And I tried all 9 green positions with each arrangement. So I've tried all 36 combinations.
But wait, I also clicked on the yellow block and the divider, which changed the board state. Maybe these clicks created new states that I haven't explored.
Let me think about what the yellow block click and divider click do. They both changed the board but the visible state was the same. So they must have changed some internal state.
Maybe the internal state is a "mode" or "phase" that affects how the level is evaluated. For example:
- Mode 0: initial state (characters outlined)
- Mode 1: after clicking red character (characters filled)
- Mode 2: after clicking yellow block (some internal change)
- Mode 3: after clicking divider (some internal change)
Maybe the goal is to reach a specific mode with a specific block arrangement and green position.
But I don't know what modes exist or how to reach them. Let me try clicking the yellow block and divider in different orders to see if I can reach different states.
Actually, I'm running out of time. The timer is at ~18 pixels. Let me just try a few more combinations quickly.
Let me try: click blue character, click red character, then move green to center, and see if the level completes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try a different approach: systematically try clicking yellow block and divider
# to see if they toggle some internal state that affects level completion

# Current state: red horizontal, blue top, checkerboard bottom, green center, chars filled
# Let me try: click yellow, then click divider, then check if level completes

result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 20, 'col': 24}])  # yellow block
print("Click yellow: board_changed =", result.get('board_changed'), "completed =", result.get('level_completed'))

if not result.get('level_completed'):
    result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 30, 'col': 32}])  # divider
    print("Click divider: board_changed =", result.get('board_changed'), "completed =", result.get('level_completed'))

if not result.get('level_completed'):
    # Try clicking yellow again
    result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 20, 'col': 24}])
    print("Click yellow again: board_changed =", result.get('board_changed'), "completed =", result.get('level_completed'))

if not result.get('level_completed'):
    # Try clicking divider again
    result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 30, 'col': 32}])
    print("Click divider again: board_changed =", result.get('board_changed'), "completed =", result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Click yellow: board_changed = False completed = False
Click divider: board_changed = True completed = False
Click yellow again: board_changed = False completed = False
Click divider again: board_changed = True completed = False
